request.question: This specialism of 19th-century academic art was among the special interests of this academic who taught at which University?

title: Thomas Lee (academic)
Simon Morrison
Orientalism
Tista Bagchi
Brazilian academic art
Peter Trippi
House of Vuk's Foundation
Portsmouth Academy building
Nigel Leask
Jacques-Antoine Beaufort
sentences: Rev. Thomas Lee D.D. was a 19th-century academic administrator at the University of Oxford and clergyman.

Simon Morrison is a music historian specializing in 20th-century music, particularly Russian and Soviet music, with special interests in dance, film, and historically informed performance based on extensive archival research.
He is a leading authority on composer Sergey Prokofiev and has received unprecedented access to the composer's papers, housed in Moscow at RGALI.
Morrison received his B.Mus.
from the University of Toronto (1987), a Master's in Musicology from McGill University (1993), and Ph.D. from Princeton University (1997), where he is Professor of Music.

His distinctions include the Alfred Einstein Award of the American Musicological Society (1999), an American Council of Learned Societies Fellowship (2001), a Phi Beta Kappa Society Teacher Award (2006), and a Guggenheim Fellowship (2011).

Orientalism is a term that is used by art historians, literary and cultural studies scholars for the imitation or depiction of aspects in Middle Eastern, South Asian, and East Asian cultures (Eastern cultures).
These depictions are usually done by writers, designers and artists from the West.

In particular, Orientalist painting, depicting more specifically "the Middle East", was one of the many specialisms of 19th-century academic art, and the literature of Western countries took a similar interest in Oriental themes.
Tista Bagchi (born ), Professor of Linguistics in the University of Delhi, is a distinguished Indian linguist and ethicist.

Bagchi trained in Sanskrit College, Kolkata, the University of Delhi, and the University of Chicago, from where she obtained her PhD in Linguistics, her work spans issues of semantics and syntax in languages in general and South Asian languages in particular, questions of ethics in the application of medical technology and social interaction, and translations of iconic texts in Bangla literature and comparative philology.
Bagchi has also been active in the area of cognitive sciences with special interests in the relationships amongst sentence structure, computation, linguistic meaning, and human cognition.

Bagchi was the Robert F. & Margaret S. Goheen Fellow for the academic year 2001–2002 at the National Humanities Center, Research Triangle Park, North Carolina, and a scientist under the CSIR Mobility Scheme at the National Institute of Science, Technology, and Development Studies, New Delhi, for two years during 2010–2012.

Brazilian Academic art was a major art style in Brazil from the early 19th century to the early 20th century, based on European academic art and produced on official institutions of professional art education.
Peter Trippi is editor-in-chief of "Fine Art Connoisseur", a bimonthly magazine for collectors of representational painting, sculpture, drawings and prints—both historical and contemporary.
From 2003 until 2006, Trippi was director of New York City's Dahesh Museum of Art, the only institution in the United States devoted to 19th- and early 20th-century European academic art.
The House of Vuk's Foundation is the name of a historical building in Belgrade, built in 1879, that serves as the headquarters of the Vuk's Foundation.
Located on the Теrazije at 2 Kralja Milana Street, it is one of the oldest structures in that part of Belgrade.
Аleksandar Bugarski, a prominent 19th-century Serbian architect, designed the original building as a two-story house in the Academic Art style of the day.
The Portsmouth Academy building is a historic academic and civic building at 8 Islington Street in Portsmouth, New Hampshire.

Built in 1809, it is one of the finest surviving examples of an early 19th-century academic building in northern New England, and is attributed to James Nutter, one of the finest local builders of the period.
In addition to housing the Portsmouth Academy, it later housed the city's public library, and presently houses Discover Portsmouth, a local tourism marketing organization.
It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1973 as "Portsmouth Public Library".
Nigel Leask (born 1958) is a British academic publishing on Romantic, Scottish, and Anglo-Indian literature, with special interest on British Empire, Orientalism, and Travel writing.
He has been Regius Professor of English language and literature at the University of Glasgow, since 2004.
Jacques Antoine Beaufort (1721 in Paris – 1784 in Rueil), was an 18th-century academic French painter.
Little is known of his early life but he had his first public exhibition at the Marseille Academy in 1756, where he taught drawing, and later at the Paris Salon (1767–83).
He was accepted into the Academie Royale in 1766.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: This specialism of 19th-century academic art was among the special interests of this academic who taught at which University?
hop1_docs: Liberal Arts and Sciences at Tilburg University | Tilburg University offers an intensive three-year program in Liberal Arts and Sciences, which combines a broad and interdisciplinary education with in-depth specialization.  The distinctive focus of Tilburg University’s Liberal Arts and Sciences program is on Europe in a globalizing world.  Both historical and current societal key issues are tackled in the course of this international program, taught entirely in English.  Students are trained to approach complex issues from different perspectives and from a variety of academic disciplines.  To achieve this intellectual flexibility, lectures are team-taught by lecturers from different academic backgrounds and research traditions.  This method of instruction is unique to Tilburg University.

Edward E. Baptist | Edward E. Baptist (born 1970) is an American academic and writer.  He is a Professor of History at Cornell University, located in Ithaca, New York, where he specializes in the history of the 19th-century United States, and particularly the South.  Thematically, he has been interested in the history of capitalism, and has also been interested in digital humanities methodologies.  He is the author of numerous books.

Orientalism | Orientalism is a term that is used by art historians, literary and cultural studies scholars for the imitation or depiction of aspects in Middle Eastern, South Asian, and East Asian cultures (Eastern cultures).  These depictions are usually done by writers, designers and artists from the West.  In particular, Orientalist painting, depicting more specifically "the Middle East", was one of the many specialisms of 19th-century academic art, and the literature of Western countries took a similar interest in Oriental themes.

Harrogate Tutorial College | Harrogate Tutorial College in Harrogate, North Yorkshire, England is a small co-educational independent school.  HTC specialises in preparing students for university and teaches GCSE and A-level qualifications in tutorial classes.  The college's admissions policy and style of teaching has made it a popular choice among pupils of British public schools who have not met the often restrictive academic requirements of their schools to proceed to A-level.  Attracting many overseas students wishing to enroll at British universities, the college also provides English language courses which are often taught in combination with academic subjects.  Special preparation is offered to Oxford and Cambridge applicants and 90% of HTC students secure places at university.

Master of Arts in Special Education | The Master of Arts in Special Education is a postgraduate academic master's degree awarded by universities in many countries.  This degree designed to develop the capabilities and resources of educators of children who require Special education, such as students with an Autism spectrum disorder.

Seth Evans | Seth Evans is a scholar and educator who specializes in the Abhidhamma Pitaka (abhidhammapiṭaka) and the Visuddhimagga.  Especially interested in the phenomenological aspects of Buddhist psychology.  Academic focus is primarily on Buddhist ontology, Buddhist metaphysics, and Buddhist epistemology.  He did his graduate studies in Buddhist theory as a Theravada monk at Mahachulalongkornrajavidyalaya University.  As a monk, he taught meditation at Wat Prayong where he completed five vassa.

Brazilian academic art | Brazilian Academic art was a major art style in Brazil from the early 19th century to the early 20th century, based on European academic art and produced on official institutions of professional art education.
hop1_titles: Liberal Arts and Sciences at Tilburg University
Master of Arts in Special Education
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "Orientalist painting" is stated as "one of the many specialisms of 19th-century academic art" (quote fragment) — source: Orientalism (passage [3]).
  - Edward E. Baptist: "Professor of History at Cornell University" and "(born 1970)" — source: Edward E. Baptist (passage [2]).
  - Seth Evans: "did his graduate studies ... at Mahachulalongkornrajavidyalaya University" and "taught meditation at Wat Prayong" — source: Seth Evans (passage [6]).
- Ambiguities:
  - The passages do not include the page for the academic who is described as having "Orientalist painting" (or "Orientalism") among their special interests. 
  - Two other academics appear in the passages (Edward E. Baptist; Seth Evans) but neither passage attributes "Orientalist painting"/"Orientalism" to them—so it is unclear which academic the question refers to.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as given in the passages): "Orientalist painting" / "Orientalism".
  - Missing fact needed: the name of the University at which the academic (who had "Orientalist painting" as a special interest) taught.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the specific academic's Wikipedia page (the person referenced in the question). Check the lede/infobox and "Career" / "Academic career" / "Teaching" sections for employer lines (e.g., "Professor of ... at <University>"). Example search keywords: "<Person Name> Wikipedia" then check lede/infobox for "taught at" or "Professor of".
  2) If the academic's name is unknown, search for pages that pair an academic with "Orientalist painting" or "Orientalism" in their interests: search query example — site:wikipedia.org "Orientalist painting" "interested in" OR "special interests" OR "specialisms" and open candidate pages; then check their lede/infobox and Career/Teaching sections.
  3) Re-open the "Orientalism" page (passage [3]) to confirm phrasing and any linked names/people sections that list academics associated with Orientalist painting (check lede, "Orientalist painting" subsection, and "Reception" or "Notable practitioners" if present).
  4) As lower priority, check the pages already in the passages (Edward E. Baptist; Seth Evans) only to rule them out by confirming they do not list "Orientalist painting"/"Orientalism" among interests (check lede and 'Research interests'/'Work' sections).
Final answer deferred until the above page(s) (the academic's page and/or the Orientalism page sections) are retrieved and the university employer can be read directly.
response.hop2_query: special interests "Orientalist painting" taught at university
hop2_docs: Orientalism | Orientalism is a term that is used by art historians, literary and cultural studies scholars for the imitation or depiction of aspects in Middle Eastern, South Asian, and East Asian cultures (Eastern cultures).  These depictions are usually done by writers, designers and artists from the West.  In particular, Orientalist painting, depicting more specifically "the Middle East", was one of the many specialisms of 19th-century academic art, and the literature of Western countries took a similar interest in Oriental themes.
Marc Gotlieb | Marc Gotlieb is an American art historian who specializes in French Romantic art and Orientalist painting.

Karen Graffeo | Karen Graffeo is an American artist based in Birmingham, Alabama.  While specializing in photography, Graffeo is also a choreographer and installation artist.  Early in her photography career, she worked as Jerry Uelsmann's assistant and model.  She earned an undergraduate degree from Jacksonville State University and a Master of Arts in art education from the University of Alabama, where she also earned MFA degrees in photography and painting.  She has taught at the University of Montevallo since 1990 and has also taught at University of Alabama at Birmingham.

Priscilla Robertson | Priscilla Robertson (1910 – November 26, 1989) was an American historian, magazine editor, and college professor who had a special interest in European social history, especially women's experiences.  She was the editor of "The Humanist" magazine for several years and taught at both Indiana University and Harvard University.

Marian Weygers | Marian Weygers (1909–2008) was an American artist who specialized in printmaking.  She was graduated from the University of California at Berkeley as an art major where she worked and studied under Chiura Obata, who taught her the ink wash painting style.

List of Orientalist artists | This is an incomplete list of artists who have painted in an Orientalist style.  Artists listed on this page may have worked across multiple genres, and it should not be assumed that all of their work is necessarily in the Orientalist genre.

Kavita Singh | Kavita Singh is professor of art history at the School of Arts and Aesthetics of Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU).  She received her MFA in 1987 from M.S. University, Baroda and her PhD in 1997 from Punjab University.  She was appointed to JNU in 2002.  Her research interests cover the history of Indian painting, particularly the Mughal and Rajput schools, and the history and politics of museums, with special reference to India.
response.summary_2: The passages do not specify which university the academic who listed Orientalist painting as a specialism taught at. Marc Gotlieb is an American art historian who specializes in French Romantic art and Orientalist painting.
